subject: general-knowledge
description: Asian History MCQs for PPSC, FPSC, NTS, CSS & PMS.

mcqs.q: The Indus Valley Civilization (c. 3300-1300 BC) flourished along the Indus River in present-day:
mcqs.explanation: The Indus Valley Civilization was centered in present-day Pakistan and northwest India, with major sites at Mohenjo-Daro and Harappa.

mcqs.q: Mohenjo-Daro, a major Indus Valley city known for its Great Bath, is located in present-day:
mcqs.explanation: Mohenjo-Daro is located in Sindh, Pakistan, and is one of the best-preserved cities of the Indus Valley Civilization.

mcqs.q: Harappa, another major Indus Valley site, is located in present-day:
mcqs.explanation: Harappa is located in Punjab, Pakistan, near the city of Sahiwal. It was the first Indus Valley site to be discovered.

mcqs.q: Taxila (Takshashila), one of the world's earliest universities, is an archaeological site in present-day:
mcqs.explanation: Taxila is located near Islamabad, Pakistan, and was an ancient center of Buddhist learning and Gandharan civilization.

mcqs.q: The Maurya Empire, the first to unify most of the Indian subcontinent, was founded by:
mcqs.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya founded the Maurya Empire in 322 BC with the guidance of his advisor Chanakya (Kautilya).

mcqs.q: Ashoka the Great embraced Buddhism after the devastating:
mcqs.explanation: Emperor Ashoka embraced Buddhism after witnessing the massive bloodshed of the Kalinga War (c. 261 BC).

mcqs.q: The Gupta Empire (320-550 AD), known as India's Golden Age, was founded by:
mcqs.explanation: Sri Gupta founded the Gupta dynasty around 240 AD, though Chandragupta I (320 AD) is considered the first major Gupta emperor.

mcqs.q: The Gupta period is called India's Golden Age because of advances in:
mcqs.explanation: The Gupta period saw remarkable achievements in mathematics (concept of zero), astronomy, Sanskrit literature, and art.

mcqs.q: The Mughal Empire was founded by Babur after his victory at the First Battle of Panipat in:
mcqs.explanation: Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodhi at the First Battle of Panipat on 21 April 1526, establishing the Mughal Empire in India.

mcqs.q: Babur, founder of the Mughal Empire, was a descendant of both:
mcqs.explanation: Babur was descended from Timur on his father's side and Genghis Khan on his mother's side.

mcqs.q: The Second Battle of Panipat (1556) established Mughal supremacy under:
mcqs.explanation: Akbar's forces, led by regent Bairam Khan, defeated Hemu at the Second Battle of Panipat in 1556, securing Mughal rule.

mcqs.q: Akbar the Great is known for his policy of religious tolerance called:
mcqs.explanation: Akbar promoted Sulh-e-Kul (Universal Peace), a policy of religious tolerance and harmony among all faiths in his empire.

mcqs.q: The Taj Mahal in Agra was built by Emperor Shah Jahan in memory of:
mcqs.explanation: Shah Jahan built the Taj Mahal (completed c. 1653) as a mausoleum for his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal.

mcqs.q: Aurangzeb, the last great Mughal emperor, ruled from:
mcqs.explanation: Aurangzeb ruled the Mughal Empire from 1658 until his death in 1707, expanding it to its maximum territorial extent.

mcqs.q: The Battle of Plassey (1757) established British dominance in India by defeating:
mcqs.explanation: Robert Clive defeated Siraj ud-Daulah at Plassey in 1757, establishing British East India Company dominance in Bengal.

mcqs.q: The Indian Rebellion of 1857 (War of Independence) led to the end of:
options: Mughal rule and East India Company rule
mcqs.explanation: The 1857 rebellion ended Mughal rule (Bahadur Shah Zafar was exiled) and transferred control from the East India Company to the British Crown.

mcqs.q: Genghis Khan unified the Mongol tribes and established the Mongol Empire around:
mcqs.explanation: Genghis Khan was proclaimed ruler of all Mongols in 1206, establishing the Mongol Empire that would become the largest contiguous land empire.

mcqs.q: The Mongol Empire at its peak was the largest contiguous land empire, stretching from:
mcqs.explanation: The Mongol Empire at its peak stretched from the Pacific coast of China to Eastern Europe, covering about 24 million square km.

mcqs.q: Kublai Khan, grandson of Genghis Khan, established which dynasty in China?
mcqs.explanation: Kublai Khan established the Yuan dynasty in China in 1271, becoming the first non-Chinese emperor to rule all of China.

mcqs.q: Timur (Tamerlane) sacked Delhi in 1398 and was a Central Asian conqueror from:
mcqs.explanation: Timur was based in Samarkand (modern Uzbekistan) and launched devastating campaigns across Asia, including the sacking of Delhi in 1398.

mcqs.q: The Silk Road trade routes connected China to the Mediterranean, facilitating exchange of:
mcqs.explanation: The Silk Road facilitated exchange of goods (silk, spices), ideas, religions (Buddhism, Islam), and technologies between East and West.

mcqs.q: Samarkand and Bukhara, major Silk Road cities, are located in present-day:
mcqs.explanation: Samarkand and Bukhara are ancient Silk Road cities in present-day Uzbekistan, historically centers of Islamic scholarship and trade.

mcqs.q: The Great Wall of China was primarily built to defend against invasions from:
mcqs.explanation: The Great Wall was built over centuries primarily to defend against northern nomadic invaders like the Xiongnu and later the Mongols.

mcqs.q: The first Emperor of unified China, Qin Shi Huang, standardized writing, currency, and weights during the:
mcqs.explanation: Qin Shi Huang unified China and standardized its systems during the Qin dynasty (221-206 BC). He also began the Great Wall.

mcqs.q: The Terracotta Army, a collection of thousands of life-size clay soldiers, was buried with:
mcqs.explanation: The Terracotta Army was buried with Emperor Qin Shi Huang (died 210 BC) to protect him in the afterlife.

mcqs.q: The Ming dynasty, known for the Forbidden City and maritime expeditions, ruled China from:
mcqs.explanation: The Ming dynasty ruled China from 1368 to 1644, building the Forbidden City in Beijing and sponsoring Zheng He's voyages.

mcqs.q: The Opium Wars (1839-1842, 1856-1860) were fought between China and:
mcqs.explanation: The Opium Wars were fought between China and Britain (joined by France in the Second Opium War) over trade and opium imports.

mcqs.q: The Boxer Rebellion (1899-1901) was an anti-foreign uprising in:
mcqs.explanation: The Boxer Rebellion was an anti-foreign, anti-Christian uprising in China that was suppressed by an eight-nation alliance.

mcqs.q: The Republic of China was established after the fall of the Qing dynasty in:
mcqs.explanation: The Republic of China was established on 1 January 1912 after the Xinhai Revolution overthrew the Qing dynasty.

mcqs.q: The People's Republic of China was proclaimed by Mao Zedong on:
mcqs.explanation: Mao Zedong proclaimed the People's Republic of China on 1 October 1949 in Beijing after defeating the Nationalists.

mcqs.q: The Long March (1934-1935), a military retreat of Chinese Communists, covered approximately:
mcqs.explanation: The Long March covered about 9,000 km as the Chinese Communist Red Army retreated from Nationalist forces, solidifying Mao's leadership.

mcqs.q: The Chinese Cultural Revolution, launched by Mao Zedong to reassert communist ideology, occurred during:
mcqs.explanation: The Cultural Revolution (1966-1976) was a socio-political movement launched by Mao that caused widespread upheaval across China.

mcqs.q: Tiananmen Square protests, demanding democratic reforms in China, were suppressed in:
mcqs.explanation: The Tiananmen Square protests were violently suppressed by the Chinese military on 4 June 1989.

mcqs.q: The Meiji Restoration (1868) ended the Tokugawa Shogunate and restored power to:
mcqs.explanation: The Meiji Restoration of 1868 ended over 250 years of Tokugawa Shogunate rule and restored imperial authority under Emperor Meiji.

mcqs.q: Japan's rapid modernization after the Meiji Restoration (1868) was modeled on:
mcqs.explanation: Japan rapidly modernized by adopting Western military, industrial, educational, and governmental systems after the Meiji Restoration.

mcqs.q: The Russo-Japanese War (1904-1905), the first modern war won by an Asian power over a European one, was won by:
mcqs.explanation: Japan's victory over Russia in 1905 shocked the world and established Japan as a major military power.

mcqs.q: Japan attacked Pearl Harbor, bringing the USA into World War II, on:
mcqs.explanation: Japan's surprise attack on Pearl Harbor on 7 December 1941 destroyed much of the US Pacific Fleet and brought America into WWII.

mcqs.q: The atomic bomb was dropped on Hiroshima on:
mcqs.explanation: The US dropped the first atomic bomb ('Little Boy') on Hiroshima on 6 August 1945, killing approximately 80,000 people instantly.

mcqs.q: The second atomic bomb was dropped on Nagasaki on:
mcqs.explanation: The second atomic bomb ('Fat Man') was dropped on Nagasaki on 9 August 1945, leading to Japan's surrender.

mcqs.q: Japan formally surrendered, ending World War II, on:
mcqs.explanation: Japan announced its surrender on 15 August 1945 (V-J Day). The formal signing ceremony was on 2 September 1945 aboard the USS Missouri.

mcqs.q: The East India Company established its first factory in India at:
mcqs.explanation: The British East India Company established its first trading factory at Surat in western India in 1613.

mcqs.q: The Jallianwala Bagh massacre, where British troops fired on peaceful protesters, occurred in:
mcqs.explanation: British troops under General Dyer fired on unarmed civilians at Jallianwala Bagh in Amritsar on 13 April 1919, killing hundreds.

mcqs.q: Mahatma Gandhi led the Indian independence movement using the principle of:
mcqs.explanation: Gandhi pioneered Satyagraha (non-violent resistance) as the primary method of the Indian independence movement against British rule.

mcqs.q: The Salt March (Dandi March) led by Gandhi in 1930 protested against:
mcqs.explanation: Gandhi led the Salt March from Sabarmati to Dandi in 1930, protesting the British monopoly on salt production and taxation.

mcqs.q: The partition of British India into India and Pakistan took place on:
mcqs.explanation: British India was partitioned on 14-15 August 1947. Pakistan gained independence on 14 August and India on 15 August.

mcqs.q: East Pakistan became Bangladesh after a war of independence in:
mcqs.explanation: East Pakistan seceded and became Bangladesh on 16 December 1971 after a nine-month liberation war, with Indian military support.

mcqs.q: The Korean War (1950-1953) was fought between:
options: North Korea (backed by China/USSR) and South Korea (backed by USA/UN)
mcqs.explanation: The Korean War (1950-1953) pitted North Korea (supported by China and the USSR) against South Korea (backed by the US and UN forces).

mcqs.q: The Vietnam War, a major Cold War proxy conflict, ended with the fall of Saigon in:
mcqs.explanation: The Vietnam War ended on 30 April 1975 when North Vietnamese forces captured Saigon, reunifying Vietnam under communist rule.

mcqs.q: ASEAN (Association of Southeast Asian Nations) was founded in Bangkok in:
mcqs.explanation: ASEAN was founded on 8 August 1967 in Bangkok by Indonesia, Malaysia, Philippines, Singapore, and Thailand.

mcqs.q: The Khmer Empire, which built Angkor Wat, was centered in present-day:
mcqs.explanation: The Khmer Empire was centered in present-day Cambodia, where it built the magnificent temple complex of Angkor Wat.

mcqs.q: Angkor Wat, the world's largest religious monument, was originally built as a:
mcqs.explanation: Angkor Wat was built in the 12th century as a Hindu temple dedicated to Vishnu, later converted to a Buddhist temple.

mcqs.q: The Borobudur Temple, the world's largest Buddhist temple, is located in:
mcqs.explanation: Borobudur is a 9th-century Mahayana Buddhist temple in Central Java, Indonesia, and is the world's largest Buddhist monument.

mcqs.q: The Delhi Sultanate, which preceded the Mughal Empire, ruled parts of India from:
mcqs.explanation: The Delhi Sultanate ruled northern India from 1206 to 1526, comprising five dynasties before being replaced by the Mughal Empire.

mcqs.q: The Sikh Empire under Maharaja Ranjit Singh was based in:
mcqs.explanation: Maharaja Ranjit Singh established the Sikh Empire (1799-1849) with Lahore as its capital, ruling much of Punjab and Kashmir.

mcqs.q: The Treaty of Nanking (1842), ending the First Opium War, ceded which territory to Britain?
mcqs.explanation: The Treaty of Nanking (1842) ended the First Opium War and ceded Hong Kong Island to Britain.

mcqs.q: Confucius, the influential Chinese philosopher, lived during approximately:
mcqs.explanation: Confucius (551-479 BC) was a Chinese philosopher whose teachings on ethics, governance, and social harmony profoundly shaped East Asian culture.

mcqs.q: The Khmer Rouge regime in Cambodia, responsible for genocide, was led by:
mcqs.explanation: Pol Pot led the Khmer Rouge regime (1975-1979) in Cambodia, responsible for the deaths of an estimated 1.5-2 million people.

mcqs.q: The first dynasty to rule a unified China was the:
mcqs.explanation: Qin Shi Huang of the Qin dynasty first unified China in 221 BC, establishing the first imperial dynasty.

mcqs.q: Sun Yat-sen is known as the father of:
mcqs.explanation: Sun Yat-sen led the revolution that overthrew the Qing dynasty and is revered as the Father of the Nation in both China and Taiwan.

mcqs.q: The Nanjing Massacre (1937), also known as the Rape of Nanjing, was committed by:
mcqs.explanation: Japanese forces committed mass atrocities in Nanjing (Nanking) in December 1937 during the Second Sino-Japanese War.

mcqs.q: The Bengal famine of 1943, which killed millions in British India, occurred during:
mcqs.explanation: The Bengal famine of 1943 killed an estimated 2-3 million people during WWII, partly caused by wartime policies and supply disruptions.

mcqs.q: Chandragupta Maurya defeated the Greek general Seleucus Nicator around:
mcqs.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya defeated Seleucus Nicator around 305 BC, gaining territories in modern Afghanistan and Balochistan.

mcqs.q: The concept of zero in mathematics is credited to ancient:
mcqs.explanation: The concept of zero as a number was developed by ancient Indian mathematicians, with Aryabhata and Brahmagupta making key contributions.

mcqs.q: The Chola dynasty, known for its powerful navy and temple architecture, ruled from:
mcqs.explanation: The Chola dynasty ruled from Tamil Nadu in South India, building a maritime empire that extended to Southeast Asia.

mcqs.q: The 1947 Mountbatten Plan divided British India based on:
mcqs.explanation: The Mountbatten Plan partitioned India based on religious demographics, creating Muslim-majority Pakistan and Hindu-majority India.

mcqs.q: Deng Xiaoping's economic reforms transformed China's economy through:
mcqs.explanation: Deng Xiaoping introduced market-oriented reforms from 1978, creating Special Economic Zones and transforming China into an economic powerhouse.

mcqs.q: The Sepoy Mutiny of 1857 was triggered by rumors about cartridges greased with:
mcqs.explanation: The 1857 rebellion was triggered by rumors that rifle cartridges were greased with cow and pig fat, offending both Hindu and Muslim soldiers.
